data.title: FS KKR: Investing In The Bonds Looks Better Than Trading The Bottom Now
data.description:
Contrary to the income nature of business development companies, they are among the most volatile income investments. Sometimes it pays to trade them, but this requires real trading expertise. We rarely switch from a trader to an investor, but in the following lines, we will defend the thesis that a stable bond investment may even outperform the expected return of a company's common stock.

FS KKR Capital (FSK) is among the largest business development companies in the BDC sector. Its current market capitalization is around $4.05 billion, and its total assets at the end of the 3rd quarter are close to $13.09 billion. The company has more than 224 holdings across 23 industries. This does not prevent it from being one of the worst performers in the sector, and its valuation at a close to 35% discount seems more of a concern than an opportunity. We were bottom-picking FSK at some point, and you can see more details in our article on this case here. While this was just a trade for us, the idea we are about to present today is a true "Alpha" based on our research and is hidden in the company's bonds, which we will refer to as the "OTC bonds."

OTC bonds

FSK has 7 over-the-counter bonds:

The highest-yielding instrument in the picture above comes with a yield to maturity of around 6.7%. The bond has approximately 5 years to maturity, with a maturity date of 01/15/31. It was issued on 09/25/25 with a coupon of 6.125%, spread to the treasury yield close to 2.36% at the time. Currently, this spread is around 2.96%, and we see no major event that would make this spread widen.

Below, you can see almost all the BDC bonds, and when sorted by ask yield, FSK appears among the highest-yielding BDCs. Add to this its investment-grade rating from Moody's, and we start wondering whether this is an opportunity to gain extra yield without taking on extra risk.

We do our own credit scoring for BDCs, and the closest comparison in credit metrics comes from CGBD and GSBD, which are very similar:

One would expect similar yields:

Compared to GSBD, FSK bonds have a yield above 1%, while the spread to CGBD bonds is slightly lower at around 0.6%. It is as simple as that: similar credit risk, higher yield. To gain greater confidence in our purchase of FSK's bonds, we need to further examine the company's credibility.

Credit metrics

  • Profitability: Here, we get a 3-year average Net Income/Assets of around 3.56%.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio): The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. FSK's asset coverage ratio cushion is close to 25.83%, with an asset coverage ratio of close to 189%.
  • Asset Quality- The company has 2.9% non-accrual loans and around 18% secured debt. Close to 75% of the assets are senior-secured. Around 87% of debt investments are floating rate.
  • Debt Structure: Being among the largest BDCs comes with nicely spread maturities that are easy to refinance:

  • Weighted Average Yield on Accruing Debt Investments: 10.5%, which is pretty standard for the sector
  • Median portfolio company EBITDA & leverage: close to $115 million/5.9x. - We post this metric as a reminder that FSK's portfolio is quite normal compared to the BDC sector, while its bond yields are an outlier.

Given these data points, FSK is rated as one of the primary BDC issuers. In our methodology, FSK gets punished based on its widened discount to NAV. The market-adjusted coverage ratios and expected profitability lower the credit score, but not to the point of being out of the investment-grade category. While all credit problems will be absorbed by holders of the equity, bondholders can view this company as business as usual.

The Common Or The Bonds?

If we compare the FSK'31 bond with the FSK's common stock using historical data to determine the future discount-adjusted ROE of FSK and leverage the bond in a similar manner, we get these numbers :

The average asset yield of the common stock is around 11.3%, the average loss of yield is close to 2.67%, and the expected ROE for the common stock is 4.97%, but since the common is at a discount of close to 34%, the ROE increases to 7.55%. These values ​​are derived from historical data over the past 4 years. Then, we add leverage (from IBKR) to the OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. Under these conditions, the expected average ROE is close to 9.02%. FSK's bond has a higher ROE and much lower risk.

Bottom line

FSK is a BDC trading at a discount of more than 30%. Investors seem to see its future performance as challenging and punish its valuation by widening its discount. While we see the logic in this behavior, given the low expected ROE even at this discount, we fail to understand why, all of a sudden, the company's bonds are treated among the worst in the sector. To us, this presents an alpha opportunity to invest at a close to 7% yield in an entity with regulatory-restricted leverage that manages to post positive financial results, with low duration and credit risk.

data.title: Veolia Isn't Exciting - That's Exactly The Point
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

This company quietly keeps the essentials moving.

It controls water, waste, and energy, the type of necessary services everybody takes for granted until something goes wrong.

The company is none other than Veolia Environnement SA (VEOEY). The business model of Veolia is straightforward yet strong. It enters into long-term contracts with cities and industries to provide them with services they cannot work without.

And that is what makes Veolia so special. Such contracts last for years, are indexed to inflation, and lie at the core of public infrastructures. This creates a recurring and stable revenue that is hard to disrupt.

A case in point is the 9M FY2025 results, which tell that this is not merely a theory. However, the market is still not convinced. Veolia continues to trade below the sector median even after the predictable cash flows and better fundamentals.

In essence, investors are valuing an infrastructure-supported durable business as one that has an uncertain growth. That disconnect matters.

All in all, VEOEY’s story is not based on hype but on contracts, discipline, and time. Therefore, I rate Veolia as a 'strong buy' at this point.

Strong Operations Power Growth

Veolia keeps demonstrating why its multi-utilities model is so successful.

In 2025, the first nine months’ revenue hit €32.3 billion, a 3.2% growth without including the energy prices. Such expansion reflects the power of long-term municipal and industrial contracts, which are 11 years on average. More than 70% of revenues are inflation-indexed. Hence, Veolia is naturally protected against economic cycles and political fluctuations.

To top that, 80% of income is generated from foreign markets, which provide greater growth and superior margins. Operational performance is also outstanding. EBITDA increased 5.4% to €5.08 billion, improving the margin to 15.7%. Current EBIT rose 7.9% to €2.737 billion.

In addition, Suez merger synergies resulted in efficiency gains of €295 million and €73 million, representing disciplined execution. Retaining 38% of efficiency gains at the EBITDA level is also impressive as it enhances profitability in a significant manner.

Considering the business lines, water operations are the core of Veolia. They created €9.43 billion, up 3.9% due to high volumes and rising prices in Spain, Central Europe, and the U.S. Meanwhile, Water Technologies, the high-margin booster segment, contributed €3.6 billion and provided 10% organic EBITDA growth. Indeed, investment in innovation is yielding results. Solid Waste remained steady at €8.397 billion, which shows the stability of Veolia's operations in Europe.

Hazardous Waste also increased 5.5%, including tuck-in acquisitions, as the high U.S. incineration volumes contributed to the growth. Energy services such as Bioenergy, Flexibility, and Energy Efficiency had an 8.2% organic increase, helped by both acquisitions and increased demand in Europe and the Middle East.

Strength in Smart Decisions

The success of Veolia's operations is backed by its financial management.

Net debt clocked in at €19.9 billion. Yes, it is slightly higher than the previous year, but that’s due to €2.3 billion in targeted investments, which includes €1.5 billion of position in Water Technologies. Nonetheless, leverage is comfortably below 3x, and 93% of debt is fixed-rate.

This structure, together with a seamless refinancing timeline and implementation of hybrid green bonds, provides Veolia with the flexibility to invest without compromising investment-grade ratings.

Furthermore, capital allocation tells a consistent story. In 2024, Veolia liquidated around €1 billion of non-strategic assets and reinvested that amount in better-paying growth areas, like Water Technologies and Hazardous Waste. The Suez merger is still providing real value. Combined with organic growth, these gains contributed to raising current net income by about 9%.

Looking ahead, the GreenUp targets of management remain solid. EBITDA is projected to increase to €8 billion in 2027, and net income is projected to grow at a 10% compound rate. Dividends are estimated to increase in line with earnings.

Well, one might think that this is aggressive forecasting, but it’s not. It shows a balancing strategy of stability and selective growth, making sure every euro spent would enhance the profitability and long-term positioning.

Clean Earth Acquisition Boosts U.S. Growth

The Clean Earth acquisition will be a decisive move towards the growth of the Veolia Hazardous Waste platform, and to be honest, the timing cannot be any better.

Demand for hazardous management is intensifying. It is due to the emergence of new pollutants, a clean-energy surge, and modernized industries like semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and healthcare. Clean Earth has 82 specialized sites with more than 700 permits that complement the current infrastructure of Veolia, providing the firm with scale and agility. This action also fits well with the GreenUp strategy, solidifying high-growth booster segments that are likely to provide strong returns over the coming years.

The deal is financially disciplined and sound. The enterprise value is $3 billion, or approximately 9.8x estimated 2026 EBITDA, and run-rate synergies of $120 million till Year 4. These synergies are realistic, and current EPS is projected to increase as early as Year 2 with Veolia's proven track record in integrating acquisitions. The operation is financed by available funds and debt, with more than €2 billion in asset disposals, maintaining a healthy balance sheet along with growth.

More than the statistics, this acquisition is about developing a platform for long-term value. It will enhance Veolia's U.S. presence, make it stronger in a rapidly expanding market, and enable the company to present a wide range of high-value solutions nationwide.

Valuation and Price Point Analysis

Veolia does not have to surprise to perform. It just requires time and a market that is ready to see what already exists.

The one-year snapshot above already tells us that the market is shifting its mind. The shares of Veolia saw a 26% upswing over the last year, but the journey counts more than the destination.

The stock spent the majority of the year in a small range following an initial rally. Such consolidation following an upward move normally indicates accumulation rather than exhaustion. Sellers were unable to drive the stock into a prolonged downtrend, while buyers constantly repelled pullbacks.

Later in the year, higher lows started taking shape, and the stock went closer to its 52-week high. This trend basically means that the market is absorbing supply and preparing for continuation as opposed to reversal.

The technical arrangement is more compelling when forward earnings anticipations are assessed. Analysts now predict Veolia to produce $1.69 EPS in 2025 and $1.83 in 2026. At its current share price of nearly $18, that puts the stock at a forward P/E of 10.6x for 2025 and below 10x for 2026.

In a regulated, infrastructure-intensive business with recurrent revenue and high earnings visibility, such multiples appear to be squashed. The market, in effect, is valuing Veolia as though earnings growth is weak despite estimates suggesting consistent improvement.

Revenue forecasts support that perspective. The sales are projected to increase from about $53.7 billion in 2025 to roughly $55.7 billion in 2026. Combined with low forward valuation multiples, it establishes an apparent mismatch between risk perception and underlying fundamentals.

The relative valuation enhances the argument even more. Forward EV to EBITDA is at approximately 6.4x against a sector average of 12x. While forward EV to sales is closer to 1x versus the sector median of over 4x. A PEG ratio close to one indicates that investors are paying little to see growth that is already evident in forecasts. In the meantime, a dividend yield of over 4% offers tangible downside protection as investors await a normalization of valuation.

The case base is simple from here. Assuming Veolia just generates the projected EPS of $1.83 in 2026 and then trades at a conservative 11x earnings, the stock would be worth about $20 to $21. The situation presupposes no multiple expansion, no optimism, and no sentiment improvement. It shows stable execution and a cautious market. Even in that scenario, investors will have mid-teens total return when including dividends.

The bull case will need only a slight re-rating. With earnings of $1.83, and the market using a still-reasonable 14x-15x multiple, far less than the sector median, the stock would be trading in the $26-$28 range. The confidence in the sustainability of cash flows, further growth of dividends, and a reduced valuation gap relative to the sector would lead to that result.

Well, it is not a blue-sky situation. It merely supposes that Veolia is slowly appreciated as the defensive utility that it already is.

Risks to My Thesis

Risks are always present, even with a business as stable as Veolia.

The first risk that I consider is regulation. Veolia collaborates closely with governments and municipalities, which is excellent in terms of stability but not necessarily in terms of flexibility. Regulators may pull back on the pricing, contract terms, or indexation when the budget gets narrowed or politics get noisy. Once the inflation protection gets weak or the contracts are renegotiated, margins may tighten faster than anticipated.

Next is acquisition execution, particularly with Clean Earth. I like the deal as it suits the business and enhances the US presence. Nevertheless, integrations are seldom smooth on day one. Systems, people, and synergies take time, and in case of a cost increase or schedule slip, the promised earnings boost might arrive later than anticipated. Veolia has already done this, but scale always increases the stakes.

Then there is the capital-intensive nature of the business. Veolia continuously reinvests in infrastructure, compliance, and modernization. At the moment, the balance sheet is fine. Nonetheless, if interest rates remain elevated over a more prolonged period or the capex requirements increase unpredictably, there might be reduced financial flexibility. That would not break the story but would slow growth in dividends or constrain future deals.

Lastly, the stock can remain undervalued because investors seek higher returns in sectors such as technology, AI, or high-growth energy names. In that situation, the returns would be based less on rapid multiple expansion and more on patience and dividends.

Generally, these risks are controllable. They are just a reminder that strong businesses still need stable and timely execution.

Takeaway

In case you have read this far, the message is not that complicated. Veolia is not a story stock, nor does it need to be. It is built on long-term agreements, essential infrastructure, and disciplined implementation, steadily compounding value as the market pursues louder concepts. The basics are strong, the company continues to deliver, and the valuation provides a margin of safety rare in this sector. You're paid to wait through a sound dividend as earnings quietly continue their work. This will not provide instant entertainment, and that is alright. When you are interested in being patient, lasting, and enjoying a good night's sleep, then this is the type of stock you buy, hold, and let time work in your favor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RZV: Soft Quality, Lackluster Growth Metrics To Detract From Returns
data.description:
It is my expectation that the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value ETF (RZV), a passively managed exchange-traded fund with a maximalist value strategy, will underperform the market proxied with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) in the short to medium term, so I find it necessary to reiterate the Hold rating I assigned to it in February 2023 and then maintained in January 2024.

For clarity, the main drivers behind my stance are unchanged, namely RZV's small exposure to the quality factor (the low-to-mid single-digit capital efficiency metrics look quite depressing, to be honest), as well as its rather lethargic growth characteristics (a point even could be made that they are simply non-existent, since, as per my calculations, more than half of the holdings are forecast to deliver EPS contraction going forward). So the problem is that in the current market environment, growth and quality are the two main factors to favor, and RZV cannot offer adequate exposure to either of them.

My belief also remains that for maximalist value investors who are tolerant to negative weighted average growth rates and quite compressed margins and just want to maximize their exposure to the value factor, RZV offers access to multiple gems. Of course, they should understand the depth of risks they are taking in this pursuit of distressed valuations, more likely with little or even no reward.

Also, it is necessary to explain why I will not be advocating for a bearish view. The gist is that to construct such a thesis, we would need a catalyst, potentially with sweeping consequences that would reverberate both through the SMID equity universe and the market overall, and I see no such factor at this juncture.

RZV Strategy And Portfolio

What is inside the RZV portfolio at the moment? As of January 23, it encompassed 162 common stocks and REITs, with the top one being SunCoke Energy, Inc. (SXC), a materials company with a 1.34% weight. SXC has an earnings yield of around 9.4% and an A+ Quant Valuation grade, plus it has gained about 12.2% since the beginning of the year (as of writing this article), so it is hardly a coincidence that it tops the list of a pure value ETF's holdings.

A minor remark I should make here is that I noticed a slight recent adjustment to the portfolio: the dataset I downloaded from the Invesco website with holdings as of January 22 showed Guess?, Inc. (traded with a ticker GES) as one of the components. However, since Guess? was "de-listed with its planned sale to Authentic Brands" and deleted from the S&P 600 and RZV's underlying index, it was also removed from the RZV portfolio. So we no longer see it in the version as of January 23 (the most recent one as of the time of writing).

RZV Value Exposure: Impressive With No Doubt

As a reminder, the core idea of the S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value Index that RZV tracks (as we know from the Invesco website) is not just to offer exposure to relatively inexpensive small-cap names. The screening process is much more stringent here, so no overlap is allowed between the index and its antipode, the S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Growth Index.

This is in contrast with a more relaxed approach of the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index that does allow the overlap with the S&P SmallCap 600 Growth Index, so this is not a surprise that the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) and the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF (IJT) share a few holdings.

And RZV's unwillingness to compromise on the value front, as I have already illustrated in the previous articles, does bear fruit, as shown by the metrics below, juxtaposed to those of the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, with its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade of B- or higher being especially telling.

And the P/S ratio of just 0.65 is quite impressive too (as the level below 1 is sometimes considered a deep value territory), to be honest.

Importantly, investors should remember that such a large value factor exposure is inextricably intertwined with its sector mix that is light in high-priced IT and communication.

Growth And Quality: Multiple Issues

However, as I usually explain in my ETF analysis pieces, certain sacrifices must be made when calibrating a portfolio's factor mix to someone's investment outlook. And when value exposure is the one to boost, growth is nearly always the front where at least something must be trimmed.

And RZV's weighted average growth rates are not just anemic. The fact is that the EPS and EBITDA growth rates are even negative. More specifically, my analysis shows that 53.6% of the companies in the RZV portfolio are forecast to experience EPS decline and 50.9% are forecast to have lower EBITDA going forward.

Another factor that RZV does not deliver much on is quality.

Even by the small-cap echelon standards, RZV's capital efficiency indicators are depressed, let alone compared to those of IVV. Besides, just about 23% of its holdings have a Quant Profitability grade of B- or higher. The situation is a little brighter in terms of exposure to stocks with attractive Quant EPS Revisions grades but not much.

In sum, this is the very core of my skeptical stance: a portfolio with a 1.4% weighted average forward revenue growth rate and a less than 4% Return on Total Capital is not a place to be in 2026.

RZV Performance Discussion

I would not say that RZV's returns have always been totally lackluster. The fact is that it had a few brilliant years, including 2013, 2016, 2021, etc.

Year RZV's Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
2009 36.06%
2010 13.46%
2012 5.37%
2013 12.97%
2016 21.79%
2021 17.21%
2022 11.35%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

However, its annualized return for the April 2006–December 2025 period was nonetheless 1.2x weaker than IJR's and 1.6x weaker than IVV's.

Other issues that make RZV a rather questionable investment are its downside capture of close to 150% (with IVV used as a benchmark) and a maximum drawdown of 72.27%.

Moreover, my calculations show that its median monthly active return over the period was at -0.10% with IJR used as a benchmark and -0.49% when benchmarked against IVV. And these figures are obviously hardly appealing.

On a side note, please take notice that the data above includes returns delivered by the Guggenheim S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value ETF prior to the fund's reorganization in April 2018; more details on that can be found in the prospectus.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, even though RZV has had quite a racy start to 2026, which I in part attribute to the Greenland situation and the Japanese bond market crash that gave some impetus to a brief value rotation, I remain neutral on this ETF.

I have not noticed and do not anticipate any structural shifts in the narrative that would be supportive of RZV gaining an edge over IJR or IVV.

This means my neutral stance does not see any changes today. I still believe this vehicle comes with too many risks, chiefly related to its lackluster growth and quality characteristics.

I must admit, though, that a significant change in sentiment (i.e., driving a broad capital rotation situation from the AI economy names to cyclicals, potentially provoked by some disappointing news on AI capex or inflation) might result in a 2021 moment for RZV. Nevertheless, I do not expect it to materialize anytime soon.

data.title: PayPal: A Brutal Competitive Landscape And Changes Yet To Be Seen
data.description:
Introduction

PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) has been a source of debate for some time now, being that it basically finds new lows every month. The reality is that the financial/payment industry has changed significantly, and PayPal was too focused on becoming a super app. As PayPal's past CEO, Dan Schulman said: “Consumers could come to the PayPal app for more than just paying for things online. It could be their one-stop shop for all things shopping and finance”.

During the COVID days of 2021, the market expected that the rapid shift toward e-commerce, digital checkout, and P2P payments would directly benefit PayPal. That expectation, combined with optimism around the 'super-app' narrative, pushed PYPL to a peak price of nearly $300 in July of 2021. At the time, is appeared logical for PayPal to pursue a super-app strategy encompassing multiple payment use cases, such as Braintree for unbranded online purchases, Venmo for P2P social payments, PayPal-branded wallet for consumer payments, Xoom and PayPal World for international transfers/ remittances, Zettle for POS software and hardware such as terminals, Hyperwallet for payouts, PayPal's own USD-pegged stablecoin called PYUSD and a few other tools and services. PayPal learned the hard way that consumer preferences for individuality, concerns around personal data usage, and regulatory constraints make a super-app practically impossible in the West. PayPal, being too focused on its super-app, was not able to properly adapt to the more complex payment/transaction world. In this article, I will explain how fragmented this market is and why I think it is better to wait and see what happens.

Services PayPal Offers

I will start with its fastest-growing service, Venmo, which, after massive user growth, is now trying to monetize its services. One important disruptor that analysts should be worried about is Zelle, an app owned by Early Warning Services, LLC, a firm jointly operated by several large banks. Zelle was initially developed by major US banks such as JPMorgan Chase & Co (JPM), Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), and now it is available through most financial institutions. I think it is more convenient to pay with Zelle because it is directly tied to the user's bank account instead of having to transfer money from the user's bank account to Venmo. When I lived in the U.S., I never opened a Venmo account and did all of the P2P transactions with Zelle, everybody with a bank account already had Zelle so I found no use for it. This is a massive risk as people are already using both and it's just a matter of time for users to adopt the more practical one. Zelle already has more users than Venmo at 151 million accounts compared with to Venmo's 90-95 million. Zelle remains the largest of all P2P apps with a market share of 54%, and its transaction value has already surpassed $1 trillion in 2024. Zelle is not the only threat, as there are many other alternatives, such as Apple Cash, which allows P2P through iMessage, Cash App, and WhatsApp Pay, which is still in the works for many countries.

Xoom is a platform owned by PayPal used for international money transfers and remittances. In this area there are also many alternatives, such as Wise, Revolut, Remitly, Halo, Felix, to name a few. Reading reviews about Xoom leads me to believe that it is one of the worst services in the market as it is “rarely the cheapest option, has a limited number of origin countries, and the exchange rate margin is not disclosed, and 74% of fees are hidden.”

Monito.com also reviewed PayPal in 2023 and classified it as a bad money transfer service (5.9/10) as it offers one of the worst fees and exchange rates on the market. Negative reviews explaining "extremely poor customer service" is a common occurrence on trustpilot.com, and it has a rating of 1.3 stars out of 5.

PayPal World was recently announced, and it allows users to pay in other countries, which management says will unlock over 2 billion new users. It sounds promising but who am I to say it will or will not work? Only time will tell.

There is also fierce competition in the POS software and hardware. PayPal POS, or Zettle, offers a wide range of POS services. It includes a web dashboard for catalog, inventory, reporting, staff, etc., and hardware like terminals and accessories such as printers, docks, barcode scanners, cash drawers, etc. Ecosystems like Square by Block, Inc. (XYZ), SumUp, Clover, Shopify, Inc.'s (SHOP) POS, Toast, Inc. (TOST), and Stripe are competitors in this area although some are more specific to certain workflows. According to 6sense, PayPal ranks number 82 in market share for POS system technologies with approximately 0.02%. It is insignificant in this space.

In the mass-payout platform area where Hyperwallet is there is also competition, primarily with Stripe Connect, Adyen N.V. (ADYEY) and Payoneer Global Inc. (PAYO). In the unbranded checkout space, Braintree competes with Stripe and Adyen with both having a reputation for developer-friendly platforms and direct bank partnerships. Other platforms are Worldpay, Checkout.com, BlueSnap, and a couple of others.

I explain all of this to give you an idea of what PayPal is having to compete with. I think it is impossible and not my job (even as an analyst) to determine which of the services will end up winning in each of the areas that I discussed. From all of this mess of an industry, I can somewhat conclude that PayPal is basically trying to create an ecosystem for the 438 million active accounts that they have when, in reality, it is customary to use many services for different kinds of payments. This is clearly not working, as transactions per active account keep declining. Its services have the highest fees and bad reviews, and it is now easier than ever to switch; this can easily become a downward spiral.

Opportunities

I just talked about the services that PayPal is already in, but now I will discuss its growth opportunities that analysts talk about. PayPal does have the massive data advantage from its 438 million active accounts, but PayPal classifies “active accounts” as accounts that have completed a transaction within the past 12 months, with transactions per active account declining, less data is being collected. Although it has a large number of active accounts, its growth has stalled over the past 3 years growing at around 1-2% annually. I am not too interested in determining if a company’s growth drivers will drive growth; rather, I am more interested once there is evidence that it has worked, even if it involves paying more. Because users use many different services, data for ads is not exclusive to PayPal, so I do not expect it to be the most important player here.

I reviewed PayPal once again when it announced its partnership with Google to create agentic commerce services. Google’s “buy on Gemini” Checkout is built around Google Pay using saved payment/shipping info and it is now expanding into many other methods.

Once again, it is one of many doing these kinds of partnerships, like OpenAI creating an instant checkout powered by the Agentic Commerce Protocol built with Stripe. We also have to see how this will benefit PayPal in the long term, but with so many methods of checking out with LLMs, users will likely use multiple at the same time.

Another big opportunity I see is its massive share buyback program. With its large amount of free cash flow, PayPal has $15 billion authorized for repurchase. I recommend reading Tunga Capital’s Seeking Alpha report explaining PayPal's share buyback program. They calculated that PayPal could reduce its shares by 8.8% every year, as long as PayPal grows modestly. This is the main issue that I see with this buyback program; it only works if PayPal does not release a disappointing quarter.

Valuation

I did a quick DCF, partially because PayPal does not disclose segment earnings. As said before, there is no evidence for a turnaround, so I do not assume one. In fact, I'm leaning more towards further decline in its core services.

Even with pessimistic numbers, PayPal's implied price is still 18% higher. This shows that there is limited downside to this stock, as it is already very cheap. Although the DCF results show a clear margin of safety, I still believe that the high uncertainty from the saturated market diminishes this margin of safety.

Conclusion

So what does this mean for PayPal? PayPal has fierce competition everywhere it looks, and it has not proven that it offers the best service/product. I don't expect this industry to consolidate anytime soon, and right now it looks like PayPal is involved in everything and nothing at the same time. I am not interested in getting involved in a mess of an industry and I am less interested in determining who will win. PayPal does have interesting opportunities but are yet unproven. With so many moving parts, I am not smart enough to determine which opportunities will work. I prefer waiting to see how all of this mess unfolds. I do like the fact that this resembles a call-style bet with limited downside, also that a large part of management is new and the large amount of active accounts it has. I will remain cautious for any proven changes, but right now I give PayPal a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: I Was Bullish At $89, But At $400, It's Time To Sell Before A Potential Bust Cycle
data.description:
In August 2024, I wrote a bullish article about Micron (MU) suggesting it was undervalued. At that time, Micron shares traded for about $89, but the stock has recently experienced a powerful rally and now trades for about $400 per share. Since my article was published, this stock has provided total returns of about 350%, which beats the S&P 500 Index's (SPY) roughly 33% total returns in the same timeframe by nearly a 10x factor. This rally is fueled by unprecedented demand for memory chips used in AI data centers and other applications.

However, this has also led to an unprecedented rally in memory stocks like Micron, which looks like a bubble and a blow-off top to me. I wrote this article when I saw what looked like a blow off top in Netflix (NFLX) in July 2025, and since then the stock has plunged about 35%. This shows it can pay off to sell when investor sentiment appears too optimistic and valuation appears rich. It seems some investors believe this time is different, forgetting that the chip sector, especially memory, is highly cyclical. I remember how Micron shares struggled to get over $100 per share back when I wrote my last article and now it seems like it is one of the most popular momentum stocks in the market. With all of this in mind, let's take a closer look at Micron and why this is possibly a great opportunity to cash in, before a potential bubble pops in the memory chip stocks:

The Charts

To gain maximum perspective on Micron and the chip sector rally, I think it makes sense to look at the 3 charts below. The first chart shows a one-year view for Micron; the stock traded around $60 during the April 2025 lows and now trades for about $400 per share. This stock appears overbought, trading well above the 50-day moving average (around $275) and the 200-day moving average (just about $165).

The chart below shows how Micron shares have traded over the past couple of decades. I think this longer-term view is important for investors to consider now because it shows how unprecedented this rally is when looking at it from a historical perspective. I believe there are two ways to look at this: Bulls could say that this time is different and that AI data center demand has changed everything for Micron and other memory chip companies. However, the bears could argue that even when considering the current demand imbalance for memory, there is no sustainable or fundamental rationale for Micron to trade at its recent valuation.

The chart of the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) below shows that the entire chip sector has rallied over the past year. It also shows that this index has roughly doubled from the lows it hit in April 2025. I think this chart shows that the overall backdrop for the chip sector has been very bullish, likely creating an ideal atmosphere for chip stocks. However, this also raises my concerns that investor sentiment for this sector has become overly bullish.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimates below are provided by Seeking Alpha, and show that while Micron earnings and revenues are expected to surge in 2026 and 2027, the growth stalls out and earnings are expected to start dropping in 2028 and beyond as supply constraints likely ease and push down profit margins significantly. If you look deeper into the earnings estimates, you can see that some analysts expect earnings to plunge and nearly halve in 2029, with estimates for that year at just around $19.61 per share. This means that the PE ratio, which looks cheap now, could surge to around 20 times earnings in 2029, for a company that may no longer show revenue or earnings growth after 2029.

What's Driving The Boom Cycle And Stock Surge

Several factors appear to be driving a boom cycle for Micron. One factor is that the semiconductor industry is generally performing very well now. Demand for AI infrastructure is fueling a revenue surge for Micron since its High-Bandwidth Memory or "HBM" chips are essential for AI GPUs. Micron has already sold out its HBM chips for 2026. The supply shortage for HBM and other memory chips is creating an environment where Micron can raise prices and that is leading to expanding margins.

For example, its non-GAAP gross margin improved to 56.8% in FQ1 2026, and the company suggested this could reach 68% in the coming quarter. The overall situation for the chip sector and Micron in particular seems to be tailwind after tailwind, creating a tremendously ideal set of conditions for Micron to see earnings surge along with the stock price. However, I wouldn't count on these ideal conditions lasting too long.

Competition Is Heating Up

As I pointed out in my last article on Micron, one of the main reasons I was bullish at that time was the High Bandwidth Memory or HBM chips. These chips are special because of their extreme power efficiency, which allows them to offer similar speeds as the competitors but consume about 30% less power. Micron's HBM chips lead the industry in performance per watt. Since power consumption is the biggest bottleneck and a major ongoing expense for AI data centers, this has fueled demand for the HBM chips. This has all been great for Micron, but the competition is increasing. Samsung recently introduced its HBM4 chip, which reportedly sets the stage for these chips to beat out rivals in many use cases, including Micron. This increased competition might not hit Micron in 2026, since its HBM chips are sold out, but it could impact guidance and results for 2027.

The History Of Boom And Bust Cycles

Many long-time investors in the chip sector consider Micron as the poster child for cyclicality in this industry. That's because memory chips (DRAM and NAND) are considered commodities whose pricing is based on supply and demand rather than more proprietary factors or brand loyalty. I see it as similar to the oil industry, which is a commodity that rises and falls based on supply and demand factors. When memory chip or oil prices surge, investment in production increases, often leading to a glut, which causes prices to drop. Just like the oil industry, Micron has experienced several notable boom and bust cycles, which I have listed below:

1) Early 2000s: The Dot-com bubble created a boom for memory chips due to the rapid adoption of personal computers. However, the Dot-com crash led to a massive inventory glut, and memory chip prices fell by about 80%.

2) In 2013, Micron acquired Elpida Memory, consolidating the memory chip market into a triopoly that increased pricing power. However, by 2015 to 2016, China's economy started slowing, as did smartphone demand, which led to a roughly 60% decline in Micron shares.

3) During 2017 to 2018, a cloud data center supercycle boosted demand for memory chips, but overproduction and a trade war with China led to what's known as "Memory Winter" of 2019.

4) During 2021 to 2022, the Covid-19 Pandemic led to remote work, boosting demand for laptops and servers. However, by 2023, demand for PCs collapsed and Micron suffered its worst financial year in history, losing billions in fiscal 2023.

What Could Bring The Bust Cycle

When I think about the fact that this stock was trading for around $60 per share in April 2025, it is hard for me to get excited about buying this stock for about $400. Instead, I think this is a great time to be selling this stock, and I think investors who are buying now are late to the party, even though some believe that the party is just getting started for Micron, as this article suggests. I am surprised to see so many bullish articles on Micron these days. Where were all the bulls when this stock traded for a fraction of what it trades at now? I think it made sense to buy this stock back when it traded well below $100, and now that it has become a very popular momentum stock, it's a great opportunity to sell.

Going back to Netflix, I think this is another example of why it makes sense to sell when valuations have rapidly expanded and investors are euphoric because this level of growth isn't always sustainable. I believe this boom cycle could end in a bust cycle for Micron within the next 12 to 24 months. Part of this is based on the historical patterns of bust cycles for chips, especially the type of chips that Micron makes. It is also based on the fact that many new plants are expected to be coming in the next 24 months, and this could boost supplies and perhaps even cause a glut.

It usually takes 1-2 years for new fabs to be built and go into production, and by the time this happens, demand has often already cooled off and other competitors may have also expanded capacity. There are now many new manufacturing facilities that could be coming into the market in the next few years. Micron has announced significant expansion plans, and competitors are also planning to expand capacity.

Micron is expected to start new production in mid-2027 at a manufacturing facility in Idaho. It also announced plans for a second fab in Idaho, which is expected to be operational at the end of 2028. Micron also announced its first fab in New York, with construction expected to start in 2026 and chip production beginning in 2030. In addition to all this new domestic production coming online between now and 2030, Micron is expanding production in Singapore, India, and Japan. Micron recently announced a deal to acquire a chip manufacturing facility in Taiwan, which is expected to significantly boost DRAM chip production in the second half of 2027.

There is the possibility that another supply glut will occur in the coming years due to overbuilding. Potential demand destruction could come from reduced AI data center capex spending. Geopolitical and trade risks, such as export restrictions or tariffs, could also reduce demand for memory chips. Micron and the chip sector are also highly dependent on a strong global economy. A recession would likely cause demand to decline for many products containing memory chips.

Where I Could Be Wrong

The boom cycle for the chip sector and Micron could last longer than most people, including myself, expect. The demand for Micron's chips could also be less cyclical than it was in the past, making any future downcycles less severe. This "supercycle" could last longer than the past examples that I cited. I believe Micron's HBM chips make some of its production slightly less commoditized, which is another positive to consider. In January 2026, a director at Micron purchased $7.8 million worth of stock. This could be a sign that the boom cycle has a lot of room to run. Finally, this stock has momentum, and momentum stocks can always go higher than anyone expects, even beyond what is rational.

In Summary

I saw very little enthusiasm for Micron shares when they traded below $100 per share not that long ago. Now it seems to be one of the most popular stocks among momentum traders and retail investors. For me, this is a warning sign, especially since this stock is up more than 6-fold from the April 2025 lows. Another consideration for me is that every past "supercycle" for Micron has led to increased production, an ensuing glut, and a stock price crash. While I agree with this article that this supercycle is not over yet, I also believe that it makes sense to cash in on the stock's massive rally well before the supercycle ends.

But, here's the key: I expect this stock to peak before the memory chip "supercycle" peaks, which is why I believe it is a great time to sell now or soon. Finally, Micron plans to spend about $20 billion on capex in 2026, which adds a risk for investors to consider because it will impact free cash flow. Overall, I believe this stock is now trading based on momentum, and the risks are increasing. When I look at the history of boom and bust cycles for Micron, I believe history is repeating. Many investors might now be making the mistake of buying at what could be a blow-off top on the chart, and I see significant downside risks from current levels.

data.title: NVR: There Are Better Homes For Your Money (Downgrade)
data.description:
If you follow my work closely, you know that I analyze a number of homebuilders. About one player in the space that I don't follow nearly enough is NVR, Inc. (NVR). With a market capitalization of $22.14 billion, it is a pretty large firm. While it has been subject to the same pains that other players in the space have been recently, I would say that it has held up reasonably well. And in the time that I have been bullish about the company, it has outperformed the market a bit. Yes, since I last reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ back in July of 2021, the stock has risen only 51.9%. That compares to the 58.7% rise that the S&P 500 achieved. But since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in January of 2021, the stock has jumped 86.6%. The market, meanwhile, is up 81.2%.

Even after this significant increase, there does seem to be some potential. But that hinges on a recovery in the homebuilding space more than it does anything else. Because of its strong balance sheet, I do believe that NVR, Inc. will be just fine. However, there are some more attractive prospects in the market from a valuation standpoint that deserve our attention. In light of this relative valuation disparity, I think that perhaps the best thing to do is to take a more neutral stance and downgrade it to a ‘hold’ at this moment. But of course, I am willing to accept that I could be wrong. You see, as new data comes in, I do my best to incorporate those results into my overall view of a company. And, on January 30th, management is expected to announce financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Revenue is unfortunately expected to drop. And profitability looks set to fall precipitously. This has also gone into my decision to downgrade the stock at this moment.

Not bad, but not great

Since 1980, NVR, Inc has been operating as a homebuilder in the US. From its humble origins in Virginia that year, the firm has expanded to become one of the largest homebuilders in the nation. In fact, the company operates in 36 metropolitan areas spread across 16 states and Washington, DC. Overwhelmingly, it focuses on the construction and sale of single family detached homes, townhomes, and condominium buildings. If you aren't familiar with the company's name, you probably are familiar with one of its subsidiaries. The one that I am most familiar with is Ryan Homes. But it also owns NVHomes and Heartland Homes.

Over the last few years now, the financial performance has been mixed for NVR, Inc. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue essentially flatlined during this time, though there was a small dip in 2023. This drop was driven by a decline in the number of homes delivered from 22,732 to 20,662. But in 2024, there was a recovery to 22,836. The average price of a home delivered, however, fell slightly from $454,300 to $450,700. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue did fall ever so slightly to $7.61 billion from the $7.68 billion reported the previous year. This was driven by a rather sizable drop in the number of homes delivered, from 16,656 to 16,247. But fortunately, a small increase in pricing from $451,000 to $459,100 helped to offset this somewhat.

For those not familiar with what's going on in the housing space, the picture has been brutal. As I have detailed in other articles, there has been a lot of pain in the space driven by inflationary pressures and high interest rates aimed at combating them. The end result has been a contraction in the number of homes being built today. And it has caused many homebuilders to lower prices in one way or another. Many of them do this through incentives. But there have been strategies that some homebuilders have implemented in order to challenge this lower-priced environment. For instance, the recent uptick in pricing that we saw seems to have been the result of a backlog as far back as 2024 that has yet to be completed, plus there has been a push by the company in the direction of higher-priced communities that it builds in. This was seen, for instance, when it came to its North East segment, as well as its Mid East segment.

Still, the drop in revenue has proven to be problematic when it comes to profitability. Net income in the first nine months of 2025 was only $976 million. That compares to the $1.24 billion reported a year earlier. Other profitability metrics have come under pressure also. Operating cash flow, for instance, dropped from $737.4 million to $681.6 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $1.19 billion to $1.08 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $1.58 billion to $1.33 billion.

There are some other metrics that we should be looking at when evaluating a company such as this. For instance, the number of net new orders being logged by the company is on the decline. After peaking in 2024, it has experienced a drop. During the first nine months of last year, it amounted to 15,459 homes. That was down from the 17,766 homes reported a year earlier. This conforms with broader economic data.

According to the US Census Bureau, which currently only has data through October of last year, building permits were down 1.1% year over year for that month. Housing starts were down even more at 7.8%. And even though it's not terribly relevant in this context, I would like to add that housing completions had dropped 15.3%. Management has been able to offset some of this pain ever so slightly with an increase in the average price per home that's ordered. But that increase is small. We saw a rise from $454,700 to $456,800. And that's almost certainly because of the pass-through of tariff related expenses and the company pushing to higher-priced communities.

Backlog is more telling. According to management, the company ended the third quarter of 2025 with 9,165 homes in backlog. That represented a drop of 19.2% compared to the 11,339 homes that it had a year earlier. This is comparable, in fact, with the number of homes in backlog in 2022. And on top of this, ever since 2023, we have seen cancellation rates climb. They went from 12.8% that year to 14.2% the year after. And for the first nine months of 2025, the 17.1% reading that we got exceeded the 13.5% reported the previous year. This matches what I see on the consumer side of the economy. Even though home prices have dropped and interest rates are falling, consumers are challenged right now. So you would fully expect to see more customers cancel their orders. And if I am right about the US nearing a recession, we should expect the picture to worsen from here.

If we project out results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year based on what management achieved during the first nine months, we would get net profits for the year of $1.34 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $1.52 billion, and EBITDA of $1.83 billion. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced. These aren't bad multiples, but they do put the company more in the fair value range as I see it. And as the chart below illustrates, compared to other similar firms, the stock is pricey. In fact, on both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it ended up being the most expensive of the group. And three of the five firms were cheaper than they are on a price-to-operating cash flow basis.

When it comes to the upcoming quarterly release, which would cover the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, analysts are not terribly enthusiastic. Revenue is currently forecasted to fall from $2.85 billion in 2024 to $2.42 billion in 2025. A decline in the number of homes delivered will almost certainly be to blame here. And with this drop should come a decline in profits also. The expectation is a decline in net income from $139.93 per share to $107.89 per share. This would mean a drop in net profits from $457.4 million to $329.2 million. And even though analysts have not provided estimates when it comes to other profitability metrics, a decline in those is highly likely.

Takeaway

All of the data available today suggests that further weakening is likely. One positive thing about the company is that it has a net cash position of $1.11 billion, which should afford it some flexibility during these tough times. But that alone is not enough to justify a bullish stance, in my opinion. Instead, because of the relative valuation of the company, I think that assigning it a ‘hold’ rating is the right choice right now.

data.title: Is An Official U.S. Gold Revaluation Coming?
data.description:
Editor's Note: Originally published at tsi-blog.com on January 28, 2026

[This blog post is an excerpt from a commentary published about two weeks ago at www.speculative-investor.com]

During February-March of last year, there was talk of the US government revaluing the gold bullion that currently is an asset on the balance sheet of the Fed. The asset was being valued at $11B, or only US$42.22/ounce, whereas the market value of the asset at that time was around US$770B.

The situation today is that the asset is still being valued at only US$11B, whereas its market value is now around US$1.2 trillion. This means that bringing the asset’s official valuation in line with its market value would now result in the addition of more than $1 trillion to the Treasury’s account at the Fed (the Treasury General Account, or TGA). Is this likely to happen anytime soon?

When we addressed this topic in February of last year, our answer to the above question was “no.” The reason, in a nutshell, was that while the monetary injection would give the economy a short-term boost, it would have longer-term negative effects, including higher price inflation.

We concluded that due to the short-term nature of any positive effect on the economy, from a purely political perspective, it would make more sense to implement the revaluation during the final year of the presidential term than during the first year.

Due to the looming importance to Trump of the November 2026 midterm elections, we now think that the revaluation could happen within the next few months.

If the midterm elections result in the Democratic Party gaining control of the House, the path ahead for Trump would become far more difficult. Not only would it be harder for him to pursue his policy agenda, but also he probably would be impeached for a third time.

Creating the impression that the economy was strong or at least improving during the months leading up to the election would reduce the risk of this happening. In this respect, we can’t think of anything realistic that would be as effective as a $1 trillion monetary injection that did not have to be financed via debt.

Adding a trillion new dollars to the TGA would be short-term bullish for almost everything except the US$. It would be bullish for the stock market, gold, commodities, bitcoin, and economic statistics such as GDP.

It even would be bullish for the Treasury market, because it would enable the US government to spend a lot of money without issuing new debt. In fact, it’s possible that some of the money would be used to repurchase existing Treasury debt, thus lowering interest rates across the economy for a while.

Beyond the short term, it would be bullish for gold and commodities, but very bearish for Treasury bonds and the economy. However, that would be a 2027 story.

data.title: Kornit Digital: On The Edge Of The Fashion Industry's Evolution
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome JB Analytic Labs as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I would like to begin the article with a quote from one of Kornit's (KRNT) customers.

"In the world of online apparel, speed is not just important - it's THE competitive advantage necessary to achieve breakthrough growth. That's why we aim to deliver 99 percent of our orders within seven days. Kornit has helped some of the biggest brands and online retailers transform their business with on-demand, digital production. With such a history of proven technology leadership, it only makes sense to firmly base our DTG business on the power of Kornit." Kevin Nguyen, Printdash CEO 3.9.2025

Investment Thesis

The fashion industry is changing its practices, shifting production closer to the point of sale. At the same time, customers want more personalized collections (influencers have their own small clothing brands). From this trend shift, Kornit Digital Ltd. (KRNT) will benefit as it offers a complete ecosystem (hardware, software, service) for apparel printing. The company has made three acquisitions, with two of them being quickly and fully integrated, thereby solving their shortcomings (complicated software, poor print durability). The result of the third acquisition will be presented at the beginning of the next year.

From today's perspective, they executed an excellent capital raise at the moment of overvaluation in 2020/21 (P/S ratio = 23.4). The company behaves responsibly — Kornit doesn't throw the cash around, they don't make excessive buybacks or acquisitions, or pay dividends. In my view, a company that has not yet reached sustainable net profitability should be managed exactly this way until profits are firmly established. The company is growing year-over-year in profitability, and so far I have not been able to identify real high-end competition in their specialization — mostly low-end small-series solutions without the ecosystem advantage. Large companies like HP Inc. (HPQ), Seiko Epson Corporation (SEKEY), and Brother Industries, Ltd. (BRTHF) doesn't have anything comparable (among the mentioned companies, only HP talks about digital transformation, but they actually compete in a slightly different sector: label print).

Kornit Digital printers will probably not replace screen printing for the cheapest clothing, but brands like Guess and Calvin Klein, which is owned by PHV Corp. (PVH), will push for more flexible production with lower stockpiles. And as soon as one supplier can deliver "just in time" and at a lower cost thanks to these machines (fewer staff), other suppliers will also have to acquire these printers.

I like their business model inspired by Xerox Holding Corporation (XRX) or Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (RYCEY): low margin on product with high margin for service contracts. As a bonus, there is hidden significant change behind the balance sheet. While we can see relative stagnation in revenues, we see YoY growth in gross margins in spite of the weak dollar effect. I attribute this effect to the move from a classic business model to AIC (one time payment vs recurring revenue). The CEO also states that they will recognize the AIC revenue from 6-12 months after they shipped the machine, and still this year we see "just stagnation."

I feel the fashion industry is shifting, and Kornit is positioned to benefit over competitors with superior products and its All-Inclusive-Click approach, which I'll demonstrate in this article. I rate it a Buy.

Description of the Company

Kornit Digital is an Israeli company manufacturing and selling machines for textile digital printing. Essentially, they manufacture three important products:

  • Apollo (400 t-shirts per hour): it competes with mass clothing production, where screen-printing technology has not been surpassed in the last 50 years, just partial automation evolved. I recommend checking some videos of the machine
  • Atlas: a smaller and cheaper version of Apollo mentioned above. Over the years, they have managed to increase throughput from 90 to 150 t-shirts per hour. Older versions of these machines can be upgraded to higher performance (and many did during 2H2024-1H2025).
  • Presto: printing entire fabric rolls (raw materials), which are then cut and manufactured into highly individualized products (sofa cushions, extravagant fashion show clothing, etc.).

History of the Business

The founder sits on the board (since 2001), and the board chairman has served since 2011. The CEO has been with the company since 2019. He used to be a military pilot, he ended his career with the rank of Major in the Israeli Air Force. Subsequently worked for 18 years at HP Indigo, from which he left as Vice President for the Indo-Pacific region (HP Indigo is one of main competitors).

In 2017, Kornit completed its IPO and gained Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) as a significant customer. Through warrants with a $13 strike price, Amazon acquired a 10% stake in the company. In 2020, Amazon acquired warrants for another 10% of the company ($60 strike) with a commitment to $400 million in revenue, which was the reason for rocket growth in the stock's turnover and share price. During this period, Kornit diluted shareholders at valuation of P/E around 400x and the stock price at $150, raising $417 million. In 2022, there was a drop in revenues, the company began burning cash again, and in October 2023 war broke out in Israel. Amazon exercised the first batch of warrants; the second will likely expire in January of this year with no effect. It should be noted that Amazon remains the largest customer; they have been in use of their products since 2017 for printing merchandise for influencers. Although it's influence as a percent of Kornit's sales has significantly decreased this year as new customers were announced.

Acquisitions

The company has made three acquisitions during its existence:

  • August 2020: Custom Gateway (cloud workflow software) for $17 million, which is fully integrated into their software solutions.
  • September 2021: Voxel8 (3D printing of shoe soles) for an estimated cost of $25-40 million. In Q1 2026, the company will introduce the machine for footwear production.
  • April 2022: Tecoma (German dryers) for an estimated cost of $8-15 million. There is immediate integration into the Apollo system, likely solving the problem of color fading after washing.

The effect of these acquisitions cannot be fully captured by incremental revenue or simple financial metrics. However, two of them helped the company create a superior product offering. The third one should take effect this year with potential in the footwear industry. This is going to be new opportunity, entering the new market: a $2 billion TAM for Kornit.

Kornit's Edge

Digital textile ink printing historically struggled with print durability compared to screen printing. Poor quality affected Kornit until 2023, but after the acquisition of the Tecoma (the dryer company), I have not found any comments about poor quality of the colors. On the contrary, some magazines speak of Kornit as the only company capable of competing with screen printing in terms of color quality, which unlocked a new market.

Apollo Max is designed to compete with screen-printing presses, and the CEO claims they can achieve the same output while reducing employee needs by 15-20 compared to competitors. This brings us to the industry transformation.

Production localization is the first engine of the ongoing development. As H & M Hennes & Mauritz AB (HNNMY) or Nike, Inc. (NKE) states, near-shoring is real thing started during the COVID-19 pandemic, supported by Red Sea transporting issues a few years later and finished by President Trump's tariffs from last year. Kornit's solutions allow companies to employ one American worker instead of 15 (mostly Asian) employees needed for screen printing press.

The second engine is Just-in-Time Production. In the fashion industry, approximately 30% of clothing is discarded because production is ordered a year in advance, mostly from Asia, based on trend forecasts for the following year. With digital printing, you can get faster reaction to fashion trends and a reduction of overproduction. This can result in shorter delivery times, lower production batches, lower tied-up capital, and better capital efficiency.

Custom made fashion is the third engine for Kornit's business model. After fashion moved to the online space, it's going to turn out to be more personalized. Today's generation wants individualized clothing, not mass produced fashion. The estimated growth for this market is 10.7% per year.

Actual Business Model

In the second half of 2024, the company introduced a new business strategy — AIC (All-Inclusive Click), which seems very successful so far. It is a copy of the model used by Xerox. The customer does not pay whole price for the machine but per use (per "impression") at a rate of $0.8-$1.3, including service and spare parts. This model is capital-light for customers (textile companies tend to struggle in the current environment, large investments are problematic for many of them). At the same time, this model allows partial tariff avoidance, as it is a service, not a product.

The All-Inclusive Click (AIC) model requires customers to commit to a minimum of 550,000 impressions per year per Apollo system. This threshold ensures the economics of the AIC subscription model work for both Kornit and the customer, although the system "truly shines" when running above 700,000 impressions annually.

If a customer has more impressions than the agreed minimum, they pay an increased price for extra impressions. The Apollo price is $1.8 million plus ink and service in classic purchase model. As management stated in their earnings call, currently 80% of revenue is predictable (AIC recurring revenue + service contracts).

The company has been sitting on a cash pile since 2022, and this is a fairly interesting use of money. Since the launch of this model, news for already four quarters have consistently mentioned huge customer interest, with 15 Apollo printers scheduled for delivery this year alone (10 in the AIC model).

The CEO was asked about the danger of AIC model replication by competitors:

"We had rumors about competitors talking with customers that they potentially can provide them something similar to AIC. We didn't see it in fact. And when we are looking at the cash position, balance sheet or the situation of the companies that they are representing, we don't think it's a scalable model that they can bring to the market." Ronen Samuel, CEO 1Q2025

After the Q2 2025 results, the share price fell due to rising inventories. The CEO explained this by saying that companies pre-stocked inks due to Trump's arrival, and inventories will normalize in the second half of the year. A weaker dollar also influence YoY comparison.

Fashion industry developments

There are two effects outside of the Kornit's ability to influence. Water use is rising significantly in Asia, which will definitely lead to higher prices for water usage. As Kornit's solution is called "waterless" compared to screen printing, this is going to relatively cheapen their solution, especially in the future. The second catalyst is new European legislation. In the EU, there is a new regulation that prohibits the disposal of unsold clothing and requires manufacturers to send goods for recycling, donation or resale. This new regulation will take effect in 2H2026. In Germany and France, governments have subsidized clothing recycling centers in the millions of euros. This will additionally force companies to reduce waste.

Competition

Aeoon Technologies GmbH

This is a privately held Austrian company, 10 years on the market, similarly looking machines, but at least for now they don't have an integrated production line solution (they need more staff). Additionally, they have more operations (more tasks per worker).

Brother, HP Indigo, Epson

So far, there is absolutely no effort for those companies to go in the direction Kornit and Aeoon are going. Legacy companies are probably more interested in DTF (direct to fabric: printing on fabric and subsequent cutting). They try to compete there with Kornit's Presto printer, but apparently they don't have any advantage except long term relations.

Why Kornit Digital?

My buy thesis is based on a very simple idea. Kornit Digital has a vision of transforming the fashion industry. The company offers a superior product, has very satisfied customers, and sits on a large cash pile to sustain its scaling phase. Its strong balance sheet has allowed Kornit to create an original purchasing funnel for companies with stretched budgets in this capital-intensive business. A quote from Printdash's CEO states the key point — Kornit gave them "THE competitive advantage" - and the only thing their competitors need to do to close the gap is to buy a Kornit printer.

The last time I saw a similar pattern, it was with Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR). At that time it was trading around $8 (YoY, returning ~70%). On the internet, anybody could hear their super-satisfied customers telling everyone who was listening that Palantir's Foundry saves plenty of money. If you were listening properly, you could even hear management state it takes about 12-24 months of CapEx to generate stable recurring revenue from every big deal Palantir made.

Good products and satisfied customers are the reasons why I am convinced that this company has a significant future ahead of it. This comment by T-Shirt & Sons was made 16 months after the company acquired the first machine, and still they commend the collaboration and machine. I was not able to find any critical voice from the last two years for Kornit's technology.

Estimated net profit for 2026 at $21.7 million (estimated by analysts at 06.2025) for a company with an EV of $150 million — this can offer downside protection. If you compare estimates from last year with nowadays, it's completely different, and I think the market is wrong now.

Management aims for 300 million impressions for the year 2030. It would mean revenue of $500 million and FCF at a base case around $125 million. If we look at the history, HP had long-term gross margins of 55-60% from printer cartridge sales compared to Kornit's 40%. That is almost 50% possible upside for the margin. Short term catalyst could be the fourth quarter's hopefully strong results, which are usually much stronger compared to the first half of the year, as stated by management. In the last quarter, before Christmas, utilization of the machines can run up to 200% compared to the first quarter of the year, so the company usually has better 4Q results compared to the 1H of the next year.

I think the price of $12-14 is the bottom for this stock, cushioned by the cash position. If I were to be forced to state any price of the stock, I would expect at least $17 at the end of 2026 if a war in Iran doesn't materialize. A doubling of the share price within the next two years would not surprise me as the stock improves margins.

Risks

Customers will not renew contracts for whatever reason, and they will return printers. This would have to happen due to cheaper competition or deteriorating service quality, not superior products anymore. For now we can see customers even after 18 months highly commend the product and buy more machines as stated in quotations. Bear case behavior shouldn't be instant; it might be a longer process, like 2-4 quarters in a row. I can't see it now.

If we imagine the worldwide recession scenario, the effect would be straightforward: production volume decreases, Kornit's revenue drops, and the company would be in the red again. For this case, it is important to realize that the company holds a large cash position on a balance sheet and has consistently low operating costs. The stock price would suffer, but the company will stand it.

Another possible risk is that the company decides to throw away cash prematurely before achieving necessary economies of scale. They could make some overpriced and unnecessary acquisitions or waste money on buybacks at the time of overvaluation. For now I feel good about management, since they have used buybacks since 2022 in periodic windows. I have serious concerns about the departure of the CFO in 3Q26, however, and I perceive such moves as a serious red flag, always. Hopefully the management will give us some clarity during the upcoming conference call in February.

The most actual risk is Israel's potential full-scale war with Iran. If there were to be serious warfare between those two nations, the stock would likely get dumped. On the other hand, this wouldn't destroy the company; it would crash the stock price. From my actual position, I see it as even better potential buying opportunity. At the end, I opened my position in the company after November 2023 (a full-scale Israeli-Palestinian war).

My whole thesis is set on superior products and honest management; if numbers are going to state the opposite, it might be necessary to let the company go and it's necessary to have this in mind before investing in this business.
